content:
The REI/Meta ad story is what happens when companies are forced to use broken tools.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The REI/Meta AI ad that ran for five days before anyone pulled it. Via Reddit/r/REIREI ran an Instagram ad last week for a Van Rysel bicycle. The bike had two sets of handlebars. The text on the frame was unreadable. A Reddit thread catalogued at least ten structural errors in a single image. The ad ran for roughly five days before anyone pulled it.
How did we get here? Well, Meta auto-enrolled them in an AI personalization tool called Advantage+, which modified a real photograph from a real photo shoot. The original one featured fitness model Amity Rockwell, who had been hired specifically for this campaig. The original was a pretty simple, non offensive ad promoting a bicycle to prospective REI buyers.
But when it actually reached the feeds of said buyers? Well it looked like something un-usable.
When Amity Rockwell started getting tagged online about the ad, she spoke out on Instagram:
“The thing is, this was an official shoot. That I got hired for. So why are they AI deep frying the images? To alter a product they’re supposedly selling? And my face along with it?”
Good question.
Meta Made Edits Without Asking
